name: Platform Integrity Signal Diversification
consequences: Immediate: Reduce reliance on Apple and Google integrity APIs by integrating alternative open‑source verification mechanisms. Systemic: Decrease supplier concentration risk by 30% over 12 months, measured by the proportion of authentication flows no longer tied to platform signals. Strategic: Shift procurement standards toward open‑source integrity verification, balancing innovation against regulatory compliance, while maintaining service continuity.
options: Maintain current reliance on platform integrity signals while documenting risks and proposing incremental policy updates.
Introduce a secondary platform integrity channel through an open‑source operating system like GrapheneOS to diversify trust anchors.
Deploy a decentralized, blockchain‑based identity verification layer that bypasses platform APIs, creating a sovereign trust anchor.
review: Controls Supplier Concentration vs. Service Continuity. Weakness: The options fail to consider legal enforceability of new verification mechanisms.

name: Fallback Authentication Path Development
consequences: Immediate: Build non‑production fallback demonstrators. Systemic: Validate fallback path reduces critical service downtime risk by 25% in simulated outages. Strategic: Establish a fallback authentication standard that balances user convenience against platform neutrality, potentially increasing adoption costs.
options: Build a simple browser‑based fallback using existing web authentication protocols to provide an alternative access path.
Develop a hardware‑token fallback that integrates with existing eIDAS wallets to enhance resilience.
Create a decentralized identity wallet leveraging zero‑knowledge proofs to enable platform‑neutral authentication.
review: Controls Service Continuity vs. User Convenience. Weakness: The options fail to consider user adoption barriers.

name: Procurement Language Reform
consequences: Immediate: Draft procurement clauses requiring platform neutrality. Systemic: Increase procurement inclusivity by 40% for alternative vendors within 18 months. Strategic: Shift procurement culture toward resilience, balancing cost pressures against long‑term autonomy.
options: Add a clause that requires vendors to support platform‑neutral authentication as a minimum requirement.
Introduce a scoring system that rewards vendors for providing fallback authentication mechanisms during procurement evaluations.
Mandate a blockchain‑based procurement framework that enforces open‑source compliance and platform neutrality.
review: Controls Procurement Cost vs. Resilience. Weakness: The options fail to consider vendor market size constraints.

name: Stakeholder Coalition Amplification
consequences: Immediate: Expand coalition to include privacy NGOs and academia. Systemic: Increase policy influence measured by number of joint statements by 50% within 6 months. Strategic: Strengthen political leverage, balancing stakeholder diversity against coordination complexity.
options: Maintain the current coalition and publish a joint risk report to raise awareness.
Invite additional civil‑society groups and academic partners to co‑author policy briefs, broadening the coalition.
Establish a cross‑sector advisory board that includes industry, academia, and civil‑society, funded through a shared grant model.
review: Controls Coalition Diversity vs. Coordination Efficiency. Weakness: The options fail to consider resource allocation for coalition management.

name: EU Standards Alignment
consequences: Immediate: Submit technical proposals to EU Digital Identity Wallet WG. Systemic: Influence EU standards to include platform‑neutral fallback, measured by adoption in at least one EU policy document within 12 months. Strategic: Align Danish policy with EU resilience goals, balancing national sovereignty against harmonization pressures.
options: Submit a technical note to the EU Digital Identity Wallet working group outlining fallback requirements.
Participate in EU standardization meetings to co‑draft platform‑neutral authentication guidelines.
Launch a pan‑European consortium that develops a blockchain‑based identity interoperability layer.
review: Controls National Sovereignty vs. EU Harmonization. Weakness: The options fail to consider EU regulatory timelines.

name: Technical Demonstrator Portfolio Expansion
consequences: Immediate: Expand demonstrator set to include hardware token and browser fallback. Systemic: Show that fallback authentication can be achieved with 95% of existing device types, measured by compatibility tests. Strategic: Validate technical feasibility, balancing depth of demonstration against resource constraints.
options: Add a browser‑based fallback demonstrator using standard web authentication to broaden coverage.
Integrate a hardware‑token fallback demonstrator that works with existing eIDAS wallets to enhance resilience.
Develop a decentralized identity demonstrator that uses zero‑knowledge proofs and a distributed ledger for authentication.
review: Controls Demonstrator Depth vs. Resource Allocation. Weakness: The options fail to consider scalability testing across device ecosystems.

name: Supplier Concentration Reduction Blueprint
consequences: Immediate: Conduct a comprehensive supplier risk audit across all critical components. Systemic: Reduce the supplier concentration index by 30% within 12 months, lowering the probability of single-point failure by 25%. Strategic: Balances cost savings against increased procurement complexity, accelerating resilience while raising vendor negotiation overhead. This trade‑off between budget efficiency and operational agility is central to the project's risk posture.
options: Maintain the current procurement process but add a clause for alternative vendor options.
Introduce a dual‑sourcing policy for critical components.
Deploy a blockchain‑based decentralized supply chain ledger to enforce multi‑vendor compliance.
review: Controls cost vs. resilience. Weakness: The options fail to consider regulatory approval timelines for new procurement models.

name: Cross‑Platform Authentication Blueprint
consequences: Immediate: Map current platform integrity dependencies to identify single‑OS bottlenecks. Systemic: Achieve a 25% reduction in platform‑specific authentication flows within 18 months, improving user access flexibility by 15% while increasing development overhead by 20%. Strategic: Enhances inclusivity at the cost of higher development effort, trading speed for broader device coverage. This tension between rapid deployment and comprehensive platform support drives the project's strategic direction.
options: Maintain the existing platform‑centric authentication but add an optional web‑based fallback.
Expand to support WebAuthn on all major OSes while keeping native apps.
Integrate quantum‑resistant cryptographic protocols across all authentication channels.
review: Controls platform lock‑in vs. development speed. Weakness: The options fail to consider user experience consistency across devices.

name: Resilience‑Oriented Procurement Mandate
consequences: Immediate: Draft procurement language that mandates fallback authentication paths. Systemic: Increase procurement cycle time by 15% but improve continuity risk score by 40%, reducing potential service disruption probability by 30%. Strategic: Strengthens resilience at the cost of slower procurement, balancing agility against long‑term stability. The trade‑off between rapid acquisition and robust continuity underpins this lever.
options: Add a simple fallback clause to existing RFPs.
Require a dedicated resilience assessment in all procurement documents.
Mandate a multi‑criteria evaluation that includes fallback capability as a weighted factor.
review: Controls agility vs. resilience. Weakness: The options fail to consider budgetary constraints for extended procurement processes.

name: Broad Stakeholder Mobilization Engine
consequences: Immediate: Convene a cross‑sector advisory board to align interests. Systemic: Increase stakeholder engagement score by 50% within 6 months, boosting political support by 20% but potentially diluting focus by 15%. Strategic: Builds political momentum while risking scope creep, trading breadth for depth. This balance between inclusive advocacy and focused action shapes the project's outreach strategy.
options: Organize quarterly stakeholder workshops.
Launch a digital engagement portal for continuous feedback.
Create a coalition of NGOs, academia, and industry to co‑author policy briefs.
review: Controls breadth vs. focus. Weakness: The options fail to consider the risk of stakeholder fatigue.

name: European Digital Identity Harmonization
consequences: Immediate: Submit alignment proposals to EU digital identity working groups. Systemic: Align Danish policy with EU standards, increasing interoperability index by 20% and reducing regulatory friction by 25%. Strategic: Gains EU support but may delay national timelines, balancing external influence against domestic speed. The trade‑off between broader alignment and faster implementation informs this lever.
options: Submit a formal position paper to the EU Digital Identity Working Group.
Co‑author a joint EU‑Danish white paper on platform neutrality.
Lead a cross‑border pilot to demonstrate interoperability.
review: Controls external alignment vs. domestic speed. Weakness: The options fail to consider the complexity of EU regulatory processes.

name: Fallback Authentication Proof‑of‑Concept
consequences: Immediate: Build a non‑production fallback demonstrator to validate feasibility. Systemic: Validate fallback viability, reducing perceived risk by 35% and increasing stakeholder confidence by 25%. Strategic: Provides evidence for policy change but requires resource allocation, trading proof‑of‑concept for immediate impact. The trade‑off between demonstrator development and policy influence drives this lever.
options: Develop a browser‑based fallback using WebAuthn.
Create a hardware token fallback with NFC support.
Deploy a decentralized identity wallet leveraging self‑sovereign identity protocols.
review: Controls evidence vs. resource use. Weakness: The options fail to consider scalability to production environments.

name: Platform‑Neutral Access Architecture
consequences: Immediate: Introduce a modular authentication gateway that decouples from Apple/Google integrity APIs → Systemic: Reduces platform dependency by 70% as measured by percentage of authentication flows no longer tied to OS vendors → Strategic: Enhances digital sovereignty but may increase integration complexity, balancing autonomy against operational overhead.
options: Deploy a lightweight middleware that proxies existing MitID calls through a neutral API layer, preserving current user experience while eliminating direct platform signals.
Extend the middleware to support optional FIDO2/WebAuthn credentials, allowing users to register hardware tokens as alternative authentication factors.
Replace the middleware with a fully open‑source, blockchain‑based identity broker that issues verifiable credentials, enabling cross‑platform trust without vendor APIs.
review: Controls Digital Sovereignty vs. Operational Complexity. Weakness: The options fail to consider the cost of maintaining the neutral gateway infrastructure.

name: Stakeholder‑Driven Resilience Advocacy
consequences: Immediate: Launch a multi‑stakeholder task force to map resilience gaps → Systemic: Increases documented resilience requirements in procurement documents by 50% within 12 months → Strategic: Builds political capital for platform neutrality but may dilute focus on technical solutions, trading depth for breadth.
options: Convenient quarterly workshops with civil‑society, academia, and industry to identify resilience pain points and draft policy briefs.
Publish a public resilience manifesto that frames platform dependence as a national security risk, mobilizing media and public opinion.
Create a decentralized advisory council that uses token‑based voting to prioritize resilience initiatives, embedding community governance into procurement cycles.
review: Controls Political Momentum vs. Technical Depth. Weakness: The options fail to consider the influence of incumbent vendors on procurement timelines.

name: Cross‑Sector Hardware Token Integration
consequences: Immediate: Prototype a hardware‑token‑based fallback authentication flow for public services → Systemic: Demonstrates 30% reduction in authentication failures during simulated OS outage scenarios → Strategic: Validates fallback viability but may raise user onboarding friction, balancing resilience against convenience.
options: Integrate existing YubiKey or Nitrokey devices into the national e‑ID workflow, allowing users to authenticate via USB or NFC.
Develop a custom low‑cost hardware token that bundles a secure element and a QR‑code interface for mobile pairing.
Deploy a distributed ledger‑enabled token that self‑authenticates via quantum‑resistant signatures, enabling zero‑trust fallback without physical devices.
review: Controls Resilience vs. User Convenience. Weakness: The options fail to consider the supply chain security of custom token production.

name: Supplier‑Independent Trust Signal Design
consequences: Immediate: Design a new trust‑signal protocol that operates independently of OS vendors → Systemic: Increases trust‑signal coverage by 60% across diverse device ecosystems, measured by authentication success rate → Strategic: Strengthens sovereignty but may require extensive standardization effort, trading speed for interoperability.
options: Adopt an existing open‑source attestation framework (e.g., OpenAttest) and adapt it for Danish e‑ID use.
Build a lightweight, vendor‑agnostic attestation service that aggregates device health metrics from multiple sources.
Create a proprietary, AI‑driven trust‑signal engine that learns device integrity patterns across the population, enabling dynamic risk scoring.
review: Controls Interoperability vs. Development Speed. Weakness: The options fail to consider the regulatory approval path for new attestation standards.

name: Resilience‑Centric Continuity Planning
consequences: Immediate: Draft a continuity‑planning framework that mandates fallback authentication in procurement → Systemic: Elevates fallback requirements in 80% of upcoming public‑sector contracts, measured by clause inclusion rate → Strategic: Secures long‑term resilience but may increase procurement cycle time, balancing future safety against present efficiency.
options: Publish a set of best‑practice guidelines for continuity planning that can be referenced in tender documents.
Develop a compliance checklist that vendors must complete, embedding fallback criteria into the evaluation rubric.
Introduce a blockchain‑based contract‑automation platform that enforces fallback clauses through smart‑contract logic, ensuring automatic compliance.
review: Controls Long‑Term Resilience vs. Procurement Efficiency. Weakness: The options fail to consider the learning curve for procurement officials on new compliance tools.

name: Open‑Source Authentication Framework
consequences: Immediate: Release an open‑source authentication stack that supports platform‑neutral flows → Systemic: Increases adoption of platform‑neutral authentication by 40% among pilot projects, measured by repository stars and forks → Strategic: Democratizes access to resilient authentication but may dilute control over security updates, trading openness for oversight.
options: Fork an existing open‑source identity provider (e.g., Keycloak) and add modules for platform‑neutral attestation.
Build a modular authentication micro‑service architecture that can be deployed on any cloud or on‑premises environment.
Create a federated identity network that uses zero‑knowledge proofs to authenticate users without revealing device details, enabling privacy‑preserving fallback.
review: Controls Openness vs. Security Oversight. Weakness: The options fail to consider the sustainability of community maintenance.
